title: Pinch and coil animal figure in air-dry clay
subtitle: A small seated animal built by combining pinch pot and coil techniques
excerpt: A small animal figure, such as a cat, hedgehog, or owl, built from air-dry clay by combining a pinched body with coiled details. The body is made from a pinched rounded form and the head, limbs, and surface features are coiled and pressed on. No kiln or wheel required.
sourceNotes: Pinch and coil animal figure technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rounded clay form made by pressing the thumb into a ball of clay and pinching the walls outward with fingers and thumb.
Scratching both surfaces and applying liquid clay slip before pressing two clay pieces together to ensure a strong join.
Liquid clay mixed with water to a thick cream consistency, used as a clay adhesive.

content.text: Small animal figures are a classic beginner project because the forms are forgiving: a slightly lumpy body reads as fur or feathers. This tutorial uses a
for the body, which is faster and stronger than building solid from a ball. The hollow body also dries more reliably than a solid mass.

Take 80 g of clay and roll it into a smooth ball. Push your thumb into the centre to create a hollow form, then pinch the walls outward until the body is about 50 mm tall and 45 mm wide, with walls 6-8 mm thick. Flatten the bottom by pressing gently on the board so the figure will sit without rolling. For a cat or owl, the body should be egg-shaped. For a hedgehog, elongate it slightly.
Roll a smaller ball of clay for the head, about 35 g. Use

on both the top of the body and the base of the head before pressing them together. Work a small amount of clay around the join with a fingertip to reinforce the neck. For a cat, the head is a slightly flattened sphere. For an owl, it is more rounded on top.
All added features should be attached with

. Score and apply slip to both surfaces before pressing on. Small coils or pinched pieces can form: ears (two small pointed triangles), eyes (two small spheres flattened slightly), paws (two small ovals), and a tail (a tapered coil curved around the base). A hedgehog's spines can be scratched into the back half of the body with the needle tool after the head is attached.

Dry on the board for 24-48 hours, turning the figure once after the first 12 hours so the underside dries. Paint with acrylics when bone-dry. A coat of diluted PVA over the finished paint protects the surface.
Build a robin or wren: a small round body with a pointed beak and two small wing forms pressed to the sides.
Make a family grouping: one large figure and two small figures using the same technique at reduced scale.
Use coloured air-dry clay to avoid painting: press patches of two colours together for a patchwork effect before shaping.

rows.problem: Head detaches from body during drying
rows.cause: Score and slip was not applied, or the head was too heavy for the neck join
rows.fix: Reattach with fresh slip when both surfaces are dry. Add a small clay collar around the neck join.

rows.problem: Body cracks around the middle during drying
rows.cause: Walls were uneven in thickness, causing differential shrinkage
rows.fix: Fill hairline cracks with fresh slip once dry. Smooth with a damp finger.

rows.cause: The base was not flattened before the clay stiffened, or the weight of the head is off-centre
rows.fix: While the clay is still fresh, press the base flat on the board and check vertical alignment before leaving to dry.
